extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator system designed to regulate temperature within an insulated box. Key components include a styrofoam box (insulated container), a heatbed for temperature control, a fan for air circulation (airflow ~0.02 m³/s), three DS18S20 temperature sensors (1-Wire digital thermometers with 0.5°C accuracy), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B as the controller. The system operates by maintaining a stable temperature despite internal content variations, with a box volume of 0.03 m³ and a fan-driven air circulation cycle (~2 seconds to replace all air). The controller implements a bang-bang control strategy with safety protocols in the low-level driver.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides monitoring (real-time temperature data via RabbitMQ), optimization (calibration models with 2-4 free parameters for thermal inertia prediction), prediction (simulation models for system behavior under operating conditions), control (command execution for fan/heatbed via RabbitMQ), and visualization (state charts and simulation results). Target users include developers and engineers for system analysis, fault detection, and performance optimization. Capabilities include real-time data synchronization, model calibration (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K for Model A, C_heater=33.65 J/K for Model B), and predictive analytics for thermal dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: RabbitMQ (message broker for communication between controller, low-level driver, and Digital Twin). Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (controller with GPIO for sensor/fan/heatbed connections). DS18S20 temperature sensors (1-Wire bus with 64-bit serial codes). PCB design with MOSFETs (current control for heatbed) and optocouplers (signal isolation). Simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal inertia models). Calibration algorithms for parameter estimation (e.g., G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A). Open-source repository (GitHub) for hardware/software specifications. 1-Wire bus for multi-sensor connectivity. RabbitMQ's role in enabling digital twinning via message-based synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Synchronization occurs via RabbitMQ with real-time command and data exchange between physical and digital components. The controller waits 2 seconds between actuations (fan airflow: 0.02 m³/s, box volume: 0.03 m³) for temperature stabilization. Time-dependent models (Model A/B) use heat equations (Q=cmΔT) with SI units, predicting thermal inertia with 7°C discrepancy resolution in experiments. Simulation models run continuously with calibration experiments capturing dynamic parameters.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three temperature sensors (T1/T2/T3) with unique 64-bit serial codes on 1-Wire bus. Centralized architecture via RabbitMQ server (Raspberry Pi) managing communication between controller, low-level driver, and sensors. Two simulation models (Model A/B) with different parameter sets (Model B has 4 free parameters vs Model A's 2). Hierarchical structure: physical twin (PT) with digital twin (DT) components (controller, driver, models) coordinated through RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated system with RabbitMQ as central message broker enabling bidirectional communication between PT and DT. Components include: Raspberry Pi controller (GPIO interface), low-level driver (safe protocol for PT interaction), simulation models (Model A/B with calibrated parameters), and sensors (DS18S20). DT integrates real-time data (temperature measurements) with predictive models (Model B's 4 parameters) for thermal inertia prediction. Communication server manages message passing between controller, driver, and DT models.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Uses RabbitMQ (open-source message broker) for interoperability between components. Integrates with 1-Wire bus for sensor networks and GPIO for hardware control. Source code available on GitHub [5] for external development. Calibration experiments exchange data between physical sensors and simulation models. DT connects to external monitoring systems via RabbitMQ for real-time status updates (heatbed status, power metrics).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics) and analytical (energy transfer equations Q=cmΔT) models. Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) via RabbitMQ message broker. Models are calibrated with parameters like C_air (616.56 J/K) and G_box (0.65 J/K) for Model A, and additional parameters for Model B (C_heater, G_heater). Algorithms include fault detection, state estimation, and optimizers. Models are validated through experiments comparing temperature discrepancies (e.g., 7°C vs 6°C with fan voltage changes).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a heatbed and fan controlled by a bang-bang controller with parameters H (heating duration) and C (waiting duration). Control mechanisms use RabbitMQ for command transmission. Safety constraints include a protocol in the low-level driver to prevent unsafe states. Actuation ranges are governed by temperature limits (LL, UL) and power thresholds. Remote control interfaces use RabbitMQ for bidirectional communication between controller and load-level driver.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensors (T1, T2, T3) measure air temperature with spatial distribution: T1 (room temperature outside), T2 (hottest position), T3 (coldest position). Sampling frequency is implicit but tied to real-time monitoring. Data transmission uses RabbitMQ with rates matching sensor update intervals. Accuracy is validated through calibration experiments showing ±1°C discrepancy. Precision is maintained via averaging T2 and T3 measurements for box air temperature.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires calibration with parameters C_air (486.12 J/K for Model B), G_box (0.856 J/K), and C_heater (33.65 J/K). Validation methods include temperature uniformity experiments (fan voltage tests) and comparison of model predictions vs sensor data. Uncertainty sources include heat accumulation assumptions and thermal inertia variations. Error correction uses calibration experiments and parameter optimization. Quality assurance processes involve iterative model refinement based on sensor data and simulation results.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors, fan airflow) to digital twin via RabbitMQ. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), fan airflow (0.02 m³/s), and box volume (0.03 m³). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ. Transmission frequency: periodic readings by low-level driver. Event triggers: temperature deviations, fan status changes. Data preprocessing: averaging of T2/T3 sensor values. Data integration: RabbitMQ server aggregates sensor data for digital twin models.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed activation) sent from digital twin to physical twin via RabbitMQ. Command types: binary control signals for fan/heatbed. Message format: RabbitMQ messages with temperature setpoints. Validation: low-level driver enforces safe operating protocols. Feedback: temperature sensors provide real-time feedback to controller. Conflict resolution: prioritizes safety protocols in low-level driver. Command confirmation: controller shares state updates to RabbitMQ server.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocol: RabbitMQ (open-source message broker). Network topology: edge (Raspberry Pi) to cloud/server architecture. Bandwidth: unspecified, but supports real-time temperature data (100J/s energy calculations). Latency: low for real-time control (2s air circulation cycle). Reliability: RabbitMQ's lightweight design ensures message delivery. Security: no explicit protocols mentioned, but RabbitMQ supports TLS for secure communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (edge computing). Computational requirements: runs controller logic (bang-bang algorithm), low-level driver, and RabbitMQ client. Storage: minimal for sensor data buffering. Scalability: modular design allows adding sensors/actuators. Deployment models: edge-cloud hybrid (Raspberry Pi + RabbitMQ server). Availability: ensured by redundant sensor placement (3 temperature sensors) and fail-safe protocols.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Development: Integration of models, simulation, monitoring, and optimization technologies into the Digital Twin (DT) as described in [4,5]. Deployment: Integration of models into the DT once the Physical Twin (PT) is deployed. Monitoring: Continuous data collection from PT via sensors over time [1]. Calibration: Adjustment of models using experimental data (e.g., model B with four parameters outperforms model A with two parameters in thermal inertia prediction [4]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Methodologies: Simulation model development (two-parameter vs. four-parameter models [2]), open-source tool usage (RabbitMQ [3]), and calibration experiments [4]. Quality Assurance: Validation through temperature discrepancy analysis (max 6°C vs. 7°C [4]). Version Control: Open-source availability for reproducibility [2]. Evolution: Model refinement (e.g., model B's superior inertia prediction [4]) and iterative calibration [4].
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Insights: Temperature prediction via differential equations [1], thermal inertia analysis [4]. Analytics: State estimation, fault detection, and optimization algorithms [1]. Decision Processes: Bang-bang control logic with heating/cooling duration parameters (H, C) [5]. User Interaction: Controller statechart [8] and real-time temperature monitoring. Confidence Measures: Model calibration accuracy (e.g., model B's 0.87 G_heater parameter [4]).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Industry Standards: Compliance with open-source protocols (RabbitMQ [3]), IEEE 1471 for ontology modeling [1]. Interoperability: RabbitMQ as a message broker for DT components [3]. Certification: Open-source availability for verification [2].

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and

road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in

equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and
pressure, in this project to simplify the dynamic model, _c_ is considered to be constant,
and not dependent of temperature and pressure following Assumption 3. ∆ _T_ is the
temperature change either in Kelvin or in °C. All the units are SI-units. Based on this
equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two
time-dependent models.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. ### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and
pressure, in this project to simplify the dynamic model, _c_ is considered to be constant,
and not dependent of temperature and pressure following Assumption 3. ∆ _T_ is the
temperature change either in Kelvin or in °C. All the units are SI-units. Based on this
equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two
time-dependent models.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.
